prompt: You're midway through delivery when a batch of costs lands on your desk to be charged this week — routine planned work, a couple of items driven by risks already in your register, an allowance held back for the truly unforeseeable, and a few brand-new requests that stakeholders raised in the last few days. Drag each cost to the fund it should be paid from: cost baseline, contingency reserve, or management reserve.

tokens.label: 'Approved dev labour'
tokens.why: 'Planned, in-scope work sits in the cost baseline — not a reserve.'

tokens.label: 'Known vendor-delay fund'
tokens.why: 'An identified risk (known-unknown) is funded from the contingency reserve, which the PM controls.'

tokens.label: 'Integration risk buffer'
tokens.why: 'Another identified risk → contingency reserve.'

tokens.label: 'Unknown new regulation'
tokens.why: 'An unforeseeable unknown-unknown → management reserve, released only with sponsor approval.'

tokens.label: 'Sponsor pet feature, no CR'
tokens.why: 'Out of scope with no change request — it belongs in no reserve. Raise a change request first.'

tokens.label: 'Client’s new module request'
tokens.why: 'A brand-new stakeholder request is unapproved scope — it goes through change control before any funding, so no reserve.'

tokens.label: 'Extra gold-plating'
tokens.why: 'Adding unrequested “nice-to-have” work is gold-plating — not authorised scope and funded by no reserve.'

explanation: The cost baseline holds planned scope; the contingency reserve covers identified risks (known-unknowns) and is the PM's to spend; the management reserve covers unknown-unknowns and needs sponsor approval. Any brand-new or unrequested cost with no approved change request belongs in NONE of these — it must go through change control first, so it stays in the tray.

prompt: Retention is slipping. Drag each factor to its Herzberg category — Hygiene (its absence causes dissatisfaction) or Motivator (its presence drives satisfaction).

tokens.label: 'Below-market pay'
tokens.why: 'Salary is a hygiene factor — poor pay dissatisfies, but good pay does not itself motivate.'

tokens.label: 'Micromanagement'
tokens.why: 'Supervision and policy are hygiene — fixing them only removes dissatisfaction.'

tokens.label: 'Slow VPN / old laptop'
tokens.why: 'Working conditions are hygiene — bad tools frustrate; good tools are merely expected.'

tokens.label: 'Meaningful work'
tokens.why: 'Meaningful achievement is a true motivator — it drives satisfaction and engagement.'

tokens.label: 'Recognition'
tokens.why: 'Recognition and achievement are motivators — they lift engagement.'

tokens.label: 'Growth & advancement'
tokens.why: 'Advancement and growth are motivators — they build lasting satisfaction.'

explanation: Hygiene factors (pay, policies, supervision, conditions) only prevent dissatisfaction — fixing them will not motivate. Motivators (achievement, recognition, meaningful work, growth) are what actually raise satisfaction, so a retention fix must add motivators, not just patch hygiene.

prompt: A 30% budget cut with a fixed release date. Drag each backlog item to its MoSCoW priority for THIS release — only Must-haves are guaranteed to ship.

tokens.label: 'Regulatory audit log'
tokens.why: 'Compliance is non-negotiable — it ships regardless of the cut.'

tokens.label: 'SSO / login security'
tokens.why: 'A security baseline is a Must — the release is unsafe without it.'

tokens.label: 'PDF export'
tokens.why: 'Valuable and expected, but the release still works if it slips → Should.'

tokens.label: 'In-app onboarding tour'
tokens.why: 'Nice polish that helps adoption but is not essential → Could.'

tokens.label: 'Custom colour themes'
tokens.why: 'A pure delighter — first to drop under a budget cut → Won’t (this release).'

explanation: Under a cut, Must = non-negotiable (compliance, security baseline); Should = important but deferrable; Could = nice-to-have if capacity remains; Won’t = explicitly out this release. Cosmetic delighters go first; compliance and security never move off Must.

prompt: Map the 'invoice approval' process. Drag each element to its SIPOC lane. One item is outside this process boundary.

tokens.label: 'AP clerk'
tokens.why: 'The clerk provides the input into the process — a Supplier.'

tokens.label: 'Scanned invoice'
tokens.why: 'The invoice is the Input the process acts on.'

tokens.label: 'Manager approves'
tokens.why: 'Approval and coding is the transforming activity — the Process.'

tokens.label: 'Posted ledger entry'
tokens.why: 'The posted entry is the Output the process produces.'

tokens.label: 'Treasury team'
tokens.why: 'Treasury receives and uses the output — the Customer.'

tokens.label: 'ERP vendor contract'
tokens.why: 'The ERP contract is outside this process boundary — it belongs to procurement, not this SIPOC.'

explanation: SIPOC bounds a process: Suppliers provide Inputs, the Process transforms them into Outputs for Customers. The ERP vendor contract sits outside this boundary — a common trap that pulls in unrelated context.

prompt: Drag the right communication method onto the situation.

derived.pushPool: ['Policy update to 200 staff','Monthly status memo to all','Broadcast the release notes','Compliance notice org-wide']
derived.pullPool: ['Self-serve metrics dashboard','Reference docs in a wiki','Reports on a portal','Contracts archived for audit']
derived.interPool: ['Tense negotiation with a client','Resolve a conflict between leads','Brainstorm the design live','Kickoff workshop with stakeholders']

tokens.label: 'Push'
tokens.why: 'Push — one-way distribution to many with no confirmation of receipt needed (email, memo, broadcast).'

tokens.label: 'Pull'
tokens.why: 'Pull — a large audience retrieves the information at its own discretion (portal, dashboard, wiki).'

tokens.label: 'Interactive'
tokens.why: 'Interactive — real-time, two-way exchange for negotiation or conflict (meeting, call, workshop).'

explanation: Push = sender distributes with no confirmation (memos, broadcasts). Pull = recipients access at will, good for large volumes (portals, wikis, dashboards). Interactive = real-time two-way, essential for negotiation, conflict and building agreement.

prompt: Weighted source selection — Technical 40%, Cost 35%, Schedule 25%. The per-criterion scores are shown; the weighted totals are not. Tap the vendor with the highest weighted score.

items.label: 'Vendor ' + names[i]
items.why: 'Weighted score ' + round1(tot[i]/100) + (i == win ? ' — the highest, so best value.' : ' — not the highest.')

explanation: Weighted score = 0.40·Technical + 0.35·Cost + 0.25·Schedule → A {{round1(tot[0]/100)}}, B {{round1(tot[1]/100)}}, C {{round1(tot[2]/100)}}. Vendor {{names[win]}} wins. Note the vendor with the best Cost score (Vendor {{names[cheap]}}) does NOT win — lowest price is not best value.

prompt: SAFe WSJF = Cost of Delay ÷ Job Size. Each story shows its CoD and Size — not its WSJF. Tap the story the team should pull in first.

items.label: 'Story ' + names[i]
items.why: 'WSJF = ' + cod[i] + '/' + size[i] + ' = ' + round1(cod[i]/size[i]) + (i == win ? ' — the highest, pull it first.' : ' — not the highest.')

explanation: WSJF pulls the highest Cost-of-Delay-per-size job first, so small urgent work beats big high-value work. Here Story {{names[win]}} has the top ratio — note the story with the highest Cost of Delay alone is NOT the winner, because its size drags the ratio down.

prompt: You are in Month 2 of an 18-month programme. Rolling-wave planning details only the next 1–2 phases and keeps distant phases high-level. Tap the phase that has been decomposed too early — task-level detail this far out is false precision.

items.label: 'Phase ' + (i+1)
items.why: i == bad ? ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is ' + timing[i] + ' out but broken to task level — premature detail / false precision.') : (i <= 1 ? ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is near-term (' + timing[i] + ') — detailing it now is correct.') : ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is ' + timing[i] + ' out and kept high-level — correct for rolling-wave.'))

explanation: Rolling-wave / progressive elaboration details near-term work and leaves distant work high-level until it approaches. Task-level detail on Phase {{bad+1}} ({{timing[bad]}} out) is false precision — it will be reworked long before the phase begins.

prompt: A change request has been raised (top box, done). Drag the next three steps into slots 1–3 in the order they must happen. Two of the options do not belong.

tokens.label: 'Assess impact'
tokens.why: 'First analyse the impact on scope, cost, schedule, risk and quality.'

tokens.label: 'CCB decision'
tokens.why: 'The change control board approves, defers or rejects it — the control gate.'

tokens.label: 'Update baselines'
tokens.why: 'Only an approved change updates the baselines and plans, then it is communicated.'

tokens.label: 'Implement now'
tokens.why: 'Acting before the CCB decides bypasses integrated change control.'

tokens.label: 'Notify sponsor only'
tokens.why: 'A courtesy note is not a decision — it skips impact analysis and the CCB.'

explanation: Integrated change control: assess the impact, take it to the CCB for a decision, then (if approved) update the baselines and communicate. Implementing first, or only notifying the sponsor, skips the control step.

prompt: {{ mode==0 ? 'Value stream — the number in each gap is the days work sits in a queue between steps. Tap the queue where inventory waits longest — the bottleneck to attack first.' : 'Value stream — the number in each gap is the days work waits in a queue. Delivery is flowing fine and you need capacity elsewhere. Tap the queue with the most slack — the point you could release resources from without slowing the line.' }}
guard: waits[0]!=waits[1] && waits[0]!=waits[2] && waits[0]!=waits[3] && waits[1]!=waits[2] && waits[1]!=waits[3] && waits[2]!=waits[3]

items.label: 'queue ' + (i+1)
items.why: i == target ? (waits[i] + ' days — ' + (mode==0 ? 'the longest queue; this bottleneck governs lead time, so attack it first.' : 'the shortest queue; the flow here has spare capacity, so resources can be released without slowing throughput.')) : (waits[i] + ' days — ' + (mode==0 ? 'a shorter queue, not the constraint.' : 'not the queue with the most slack.'))

explanation: {{ mode==0 ? 'Lead time is governed by the bottleneck — the longest queue. Improve it first; speeding a non-bottleneck just makes its queue grow.' : 'Throughput is set by the bottleneck, so the step with the most slack (the shortest queue) can give up resources without affecting the schedule — extra capacity there is wasted while the bottleneck elsewhere sets the pace.' }}

prompt: Drag each feature to its Kano category — Must-be (expected; absence dissatisfies), Performance (more is better), or Delighter (unexpected; presence delights).

tokens.label: 'Data encryption'
tokens.why: 'Security is a basic expectation — absent it strongly dissatisfies, present it is merely assumed.'

tokens.label: 'SSO login'
tokens.why: 'Expected baseline functionality — a Must-be, not a differentiator.'

tokens.label: 'Export speed'
tokens.why: 'Linear satisfaction — faster is better, slower is worse: a Performance attribute.'

tokens.label: 'Search relevance'
tokens.why: 'More of it steadily raises satisfaction — a Performance attribute.'

tokens.label: 'Dark mode'
tokens.why: 'Unexpected polish — present it delights, absent it is not missed: a Delighter.'

tokens.label: 'AI auto-summary'
tokens.why: 'A surprising extra that excites users — a Delighter (until it becomes expected).'

explanation: Kano sorts features by how they drive satisfaction: Must-be (expected — only dissatisfies if missing), Performance (linear — more is better), and Delighters (unexpected — delight when present). Delighters decay into expectations over time, so the baseline must always cover the Must-bes first.

prompt: At 60% complete the sponsor will not increase the budget. Read the TCPI-to-budget shown. What does it mean for the remaining work?

choices.text: 'Remaining work must run about ' + pct + '% more cost-efficiently than so far.'
'The project is currently ' + pct + '% over its total approved budget.'
'The team needs roughly ' + pct + '% more funding to finish the work.'
'Cost efficiency will recover on its own without any action.'

explanation: TCPI is the cost efficiency REQUIRED on the remaining work to still hit the target. A TCPI above 1.0 means the team must perform better than it has — here about {{pct}}% more efficiently — which is a warning that the target may be unrealistic, not a measure of current overrun or a funding request.

prompt: Your team's sprint to extend the customer portal ran over six days. Two changes were formally approved through the board: on day {{ad0}} the CCB signed off {{changes[0]}}, and on day {{ad1}} it signed off {{changes[1]}}. Reviewing the burn-up afterwards, the BLUE scope line rose three times — one more than the board approved. Tap the scope increase that no approved change request covers.

items.label: 'day ' + riseDays[i]
items.why: i == badWhich ? ('Day ' + riseDays[i] + ': the scope line rose here but no CR was approved for this day — scope injected outside change control.') : ('Day ' + riseDays[i] + ': the board approved a change on this day — this rise is legitimate.')

explanation: A burn-up chart shows total scope as the upper line, so every rise is a scope change. The board approved changes on days {{ad0}} and {{ad1}}, but the scope line also stepped up on day {{badDay}} — that increase went in with no change request, outside integrated change control, which is what an audit must catch.

prompt:
Each letter marks a risk's INITIAL cell (risk = probability × impact). After mitigation, tap every risk whose RESIDUAL score is still ≥ 12:
A:  −{{dP[0]}} probability,  −{{dI[0]}} impact
B:  −{{dP[1]}} probability,  −{{dI[1]}} impact
C:  −{{dP[2]}} probability,  −{{dI[2]}} impact
D:  −{{dP[3]}} probability,  −{{dI[3]}} impact

items.label: names[i]
items.why: 'Residual = ' + rP[i] + '×' + rI[i] + ' = ' + rS[i] + (rS[i] >= 12 ? ' ≥ 12 — still breaches the threshold; needs a contingency plan.' : ' < 12 — mitigation brought it under threshold.')

explanation: Residual risk is the initial risk minus the effect of the response. Recompute probability × impact AFTER the reduction — only residuals ≥ 12 still breach the threshold. A high initial score mitigated below 12 is now acceptable; {{nOver}} risk(s) here still need a plan.
